system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Module 2 - Chord Vocabulary
Pages 32-88 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Module 2 - Chord
  Vocabulary


The Three Harmonic Families

in music, there are three primary colors when it comes to harmony.

A chord must belong to one of the following classifications:

   Major – e.g. CMaj7, C6, CMaj9
   Minor – e.g. Cm7, Cm9, Cm7b5
   Dominant – e.g. C7, C9, C13, C7(b9)


The above diagram is a good generalization, however it’s helpful to add a further sub-classification
to minor and dominant chords as follows:




Minor:

   ‘m7’ sub-family – e.g. Cm7, Cm9, Cm13
   ‘m7b5’ sub-family – e.g. Cm7b5, Cm11b5

Dominant:

   ‘7’ or ‘natural dominant’ sub-family – e.g. C7, C9, C13
   ‘7alt’ or ‘altered dominant’ sub-family – e.g. C7(b9). C7(b13b9), C7(b5), C7alt



I’m pointing this out early on in this course because it can often sound wrong playing a natural
dominant instead of an altered dominant or playing a m7 instead of a m7b5 chord depending on the
harmonic situation.

We’ll cover these issues in more detail in the following lessons.

Keep the above concepts in mind as we head to the next lesson on your first chord voicing ‘collection’
you’ll use to arrange your first chord melody.


Your First Chord Collection

Alright – time to get our hands dirty and learn some chord voicings.

In this lesson, you’re going to come away with a chord collection that you can use when harmonizing
major chords in a chord melody.




What Is a Chord Collection?

A chord collection is a group of chords that can be played over a particular chord quality or
progression.

They are useful because the sequence of chords in the collection includes every diatonic interval as
the top note of the chords.

Which means: no matter which note is in the melody line, you can harmonize it by referring to the
chord collection.


Step 1 - Arpeggio

Your first chord collection is going to be major in the key of G.

Let’s start with this GMaj7 arpeggio:




   Play audio example 2-1




The blue number between the notation and TAB represents the interval each note is in relation to the
arpeggio. For example, the B on the 7th fret is the 3rd of GMaj7.



As mentioned earlier in the course, it’s good to finger single lines horizontally up through the top
strings so you can fit chord shapes underneath.


Step 2 - Harmonize Arpeggio

Now harmonize each of the arpeggio notes with the following chord shapes:




   Play audio example 2-2




The arpeggio we played above is now the top note of each of the chord voicings.

These aren’t the only possible chord voicings that you could use from the major chord family (you are
welcome to ‘swap out’ any of these voicings for alternatives that have the same interval as the top
note).

But:

These voicings are very useful for harmonizing the notes as they are easy to play and sound reliably
good in a chord melody.


Tips:

   Don’t be too phased by the chord names at this point – the main thing to remember here is all
   these chords are from the major chord family.
   The fingerings of the chords are important if you can manage them. An example would be the
   3rd finger barre of the GMaj7 voicing. Using barres in that way can free up your other fingers
   to play additional notes or passing tones. But if you find these kind of barre techniques too
   uncomfortable despite regular practice, adapt the fingerings to suit your technique OR use
   alternative chord voicings you find easier.


Step 3 - Add Notes From the G Major Scale

Let’s go further with this idea and now include all seven notes from the G Major scale, not just the
arpeggio:




   Play audio example 2-3




If you play this example, notice how it sounds like a Joe Pass chord melody line. Joe Pass would often
play a block chord then link to the next chord voicing with scale notes or passing tones in this way.


Step 4 - Harmonize All Notes of the Scale

Finally, let’s complete this G major chord collection by harmonizing all the notes from the G major
scale as follows:




   Play audio example 2-4


We now have found a voicing to play over every scale note in G Major.

Which means:

You’ll be able to harmonize any melody note that’s on a chord from the G Major chord family we see
in any song we choose to arrange. Pretty cool, huh?


Step 5 - Transpose the Chord Collection to Other Keys

Here’s the problem at this point: we aren’t just going to see major chords based on G major. They
could be F major chords, Ab major, and so on.

So you now need to learn how to transpose this chord collection into any key – giving you the ability
to apply this chord collection to any major chord.

Let’s transpose this chord collection to C Major now:




   Play audio example 2-5


Notice how the shapes are exactly the same as G Major, I’ve just shifted them up to start on the 8th
fret instead of the third fret.

But:

It’s getting a little bit too high on the fretboard now for the last few shapes.


So, let’s drop some shapes down the octave when they start to get a bit too high. This is a much
more practical way to practice the chord shapes for this key:




   Play audio example 2-6




Voila! We now have our first chord collection, which gives us a useful pallet of chords to harmonize
any note of a major scale.


Exercise - A Summer's Day

A great way to learn new material is to get creative.

So, let’s have some fun improvising a chord solo using the shapes you just learned in the key of C.

For this exercise, play along with the backing track provided and play through each of the shapes –
any of the shapes in any order will generally sound fine.

Experiment with adding extra notes with your free fingers to the chord voicings for more interest.
You can also play some single lines in between the chord voicings to mix things up.

Here’s an example of me using the chord scale over the Summer’s Day backing track:




 Task: Have some fun improvising with the chords on the Summer’s Day backing track below using
 the C major chord collection we’ve developed, as a way to help get these new chord shapes under
 your fingers.


Use these shapes:


Over this chord progression and backing track:




   Play Backing Track


Major ii V I Chord Collections

In the previous lesson, we learned how to apply major chords to each note in a C major scale.

But we are going to need more than just major chords for chord melody arranging.

Let’s refer back to our 3 Harmonic Families for a moment:




As you can see from the above diagram, we also need minor and dominant chords in addition to
major chords if we are going to be able to make chord melody arrangements.

One of the best ways to learn useful combinations of major, minor, and dominant chords is to learn
voicings that suit major ii V Iprogressions.

The reason:

The ii-V-I progression contains a chord from each harmonic family:

   ii – minor
   V – dominant
   I – major

So let’s build out our knowledge further by learning suitable minor and dominantchord voicings that
will work in the key of C major. Then, we’ll combine the voicings to make ii V I progressions in each
area of the fretboard.


The aim of all this is to help you build out a library of useful voicings that you can use in your
chord melody arrangements.


Step 1 - Practice the Following Chord Collections

Start by practicing the following chord collections.


C Major Chord Collection (I in C Major)



   Play audio example 2-7


D Minor Chord Collection (ii in C Major)



 Play audio example 2-8


G Dominant Chord Collection (V in C Major)



 Play audio example 2-9


Step 2 - Create ii V I Progressions

Now let’s create ii-V-I progressions in each area of the fretboard.

To orientate yourself, first play a C major Scale in each fretboard area, then play a ii V I using chord
shapes from the above collections closest to each fretboard area.



 Note: I am using the CAGED system to refer to each area of the fretboard. For more information
 on understanding the CAGED system, check out this lesson first.



Let’s give that a go.




CAGED “A” Position



   Play audio example 2-10


CAGED “G” Position



 Play audio example 2-11




CAGED “E” Position



 Play audio example 2-12


CAGED “D/C” Position



 Play audio example 2-13


Step 3 - Practice Each of the ii V I Chord Combinations

Practice each of the ii V I chord combinations you learned above over this backing track to get used to
moving through each chord.

Practice each fretboard area until comfortable before moving to the next:




   Play backing track (slow tempo)




   Play backing track (medium tempo)


Step 4 - "2nd String" Chord Collections

As covered in module 1, when arranging chord melodies it’s helpful to have the melody mainly on the
top 1st and 2nd strings.

So far, the chord voicings we have learned in this lesson have the highest note on the 1st string.

Now let’s add additional chord collections that have their highest note on the 2nd string.


“2nd String” C Major Chord Collection (I in C Major)



 Play audio example 2-14


“2nd String” D Minor Chord Collection (ii in C Major)



 Play audio example 2-15


“2nd String” G Dominant Chord Collection (V in C Major)



 Play audio example 2-16




Challenge: experiment with the above “2nd string” voicings and see if you can work out how to
play ii V I progressions in each of the fretboard areas, similar to how you played the 1st string
voicings earlier in the lesson.


Minor ii V i Chord Collections

Great news! We have nearly finished assembling our full chord dictionary, comprised of several chord
collections.

So far we have covered:

   Major chords
   Dominant chords
   Minor chords

Something we haven’t covered yet though is all the sub-types of minor and dominant chords.

Let’s look at this diagram once again:




As you can see, the remaining sub-types we have to look at are:

   Minor 7th chords with a b5 (m7b5) aka half-diminished chords.
   Altered dominant chords (7alt) – dominant chords with altered tensionsapplied to it.

Don’t worry if the labels and theory don’t make sense yet – we will cover what you need to know
theory-wise in the following lessons.

A great way to learn these new kinds of chords is by studying minor ii V i progressions, as they
include m7b5 for the ii chord and 7alt for the V chord.


Let’s create some new chord collections now using these chord types. After that, we’ll combine the
chords to create minor ii V i progressions in each fretboard area.




 Helpful note: I know by now you might be getting a little weary learning all these chord voicings.
 Keep in mind that we are more building a dictionary of chords rather than trying to memorize
 every single one at this stage.

 We are creating a reference library of chords and so it is ok if you are finding these voicings
 difficult to commit to memory – that’s not necessary and it is perfectly ok to look back on these
 lessons as a chord reference when we start arranging songs. Just play through each of the
 voicings and notice the type of sound and what the fingerings feel like before moving on.


Step 1 - Practice the Following Chord Collections

Start by practicing the following chord collections.


C Minor Chord Collection (i of C Minor)



   Play audio example 2-17


Dm7b5 Chord Collection (ii of C Minor)



 Play audio example 2-18


G Altered Dominant Chord Collection (V of C Minor)



 Play audio example 2-19


Step 2 - Create minor ii V i Progressions

Now let’s create minor ii V i progressions in each area of the fretboard.

You should know the process from the last lesson – play a C natural minor scale in each fretboard
area, then play a ii V i using chord shapes from the above collections closest to each fretboard area.

Let’s give that a go:


Caged “A” Position (Minor)



   Play audio example 2-20


Caged “G” Position (Minor)



 Play audio example 2-21


Caged “E” Position (Minor)



 Play audio example 2-22


Caged “D/C” Position (Minor)



 Play audio example 2-23


Step 3 - Practice Each of the ii V i Chord Combinations

Practice each of the minor ii V i chord combinations you learned above over this backing track to get
used to moving through each chord.

Practice each fretboard area until comfortable before moving to the next:




   Play backing track (slow tempo)




   Play backing track (medium tempo)


Step 4 - "2nd String" Chord Collections

Now let’s add additional chord collections that have their highest note on the 2nd string.




“2nd String” C Minor Chord Collection (i in C Minor)



   Play audio example 2-24


“2nd String” Dm7b5 Chord Collection (ii in C Minor)



 Play audio example 2-25


“2nd String” G7alt Chord Collection (V in C Minor)



 Play audio example 2-26




Challenge: experiment with the above “2nd string” voicings and see if you can work out how to
play ii V i progressions in each of the fretboard areas, similar to how you played the 1st string
voicings earlier in the lesson.


Understanding Chord Labels

So far in this course, you have played a great many different types of chords included in all those
chord collections we’ve looked at.

You might be wondering at this point what all those complex-looking labels mean, e.g. chord symbols
like Db7(b13#9), AMaj7#11, and Bm7b5/D.

It’s important we look at how to decode these chord symbols, as it can help you not only understand
how chords work but also how to eventually construct your own chord voicings.




What Is a Chord Symbol?




 A chord symbol is an attempt to describe the type of sound produced by a group of notes.




Each symbol you see stands for a formula of intervals:

For example:

Maj7 formula = 1 3 5 7

So Cmaj7 = C E G B

The formulas generally follow a predictable pattern (with some exceptions).


Constructing Chords

Chords are constructed most often by stacking thirds.

By way of example, let’s take a G major scale:




   Play audio example 2-27




Let’s now stack 2 extra thirds on each scale note – resulting in a series of triads i.e. 3 note chords:




   Play audio example 2-28




A triad is the smallest amount of notes you can have for a chord.


We can continue to stack an extra third on top of these triads, giving us 4 note chords or ‘jazz’ chords
– aptly named because of the jazzy sound this extra 3rd gives us:




   Play audio example 2-29




Note: The second bar of this example got a bit high on the fretboard so I took those chords down the
octave for playability. Don’t try to play this example as some of the chord shapes are very tricky with
large stretches – I resorted to recording this with two guitars mixed together!


Understanding the Numbers in Chord Symbols

You might be wondering at this point “Why do they call notes 9, 11, and 13 instead of 2, 4, 6 in
chord names?”

First, let’s look at the G major scale again, but this time play it through another octave:




   Play audio example 2-30




Notice in the second octave we continued to count the intervals past 8 – continuing to 9,10,11,12 and
so on.

Let’s now play an arpeggio where we play up by 3rds through this 2-octave scale:




   Play audio example 2-31


Remember how we were stacking 3rds to make jazz chords? As you can see, as we continue through
the series of 3rds, we get into numbers like 9,11, and 13.

This is where chords like GMaj13 come from – from continuing to stack more and more thirds
onto the structure, which gives us more and more colorful extensions:




   Play audio example 2-32




Going back to our 1-octave G major scale, this gives us the explanation as to why you will see me
refer to the intervals as 9, 11, and 13 – as these are more useful interval names for these notes
considering from a chordal point of view, these notes are derived from the 2nd octave of the scale.

In fact, chords that include the 9th, 11th, or 13th are often called ‘second octave structures’.




   Play audio example 2-33


Omittable Notes

Notice how those GMaj9 and GMaj13 chords we constructed above are unplayable on the guitar.

Here’s the thing:

On the guitar, there is a difference between the theoretically correct chord and the playable chord.

Often notes can be omitted from the chord shape to get a similar enough sound, but with a much
easier shape.

So we can omit notes from the structures to maintain the essence of the chord but make for more
playable voicings on our instrument:




   Play audio example 2-34




Intervals most commonly omitted include the 5th, the root (1st), the 11th, and the 9th. Note though
that even the 3rd and the 7th can be omitted for more complex chords in certain situations as well.


Labelling Extensions and Altered Tensions

An extension (sometimes called a ‘natural’ or ‘diatonic’ tension) is an interval of the chord which
naturally occurs in the parent scale of the key of the progression. Extensions are included as part of
the chord name. Examples are G9, G13, and GMaj9.

Altered tensions are notes that are outside the parent scale of the key of the progression, and are
(usually) added in parentheses after the chord name. Examples are GMaj7(#11), G7(b13#9).



 Exercise: play the following chord voicings and see if you can work out which ones have
 extensions and which have altered tensions applied to them.


Play audio example 2-35


Slash Chords

Slash chords are a way to indicate what note should be in the bass of a chord.

For example: G6/E means to play a G6 chord with an E as the lowest note.

An important note is that if playing in a band, it’s more important for the bass player to pay
attention to these compared to the guitarist – as the guitarist can play voicings that sit above the
bass player anyway.

Nevertheless, they are useful to know what this means and can give you some interesting
possibilities, especially for solo jazz guitar.

Play through the following slash chords and notice the lowest note in each voicing and how it related
to the chord symbol indicated:




   Play audio example 2-36


Same Notes, Different Chord Names

The same group of notes can have different chord names. Depending on their context in the chord
progression you might choose one label over another – whichever makes more sense.

Let’s take this collection of notes:




   Play audio example 2-37




The notes in this chord are C E G B.

This is clearly a CMaj7 chord, as you can hear in this progression:




   Play audio example 2-38


But – it could also be an Am9 chord (with the A root omitted) if you place it in this progression
instead:




   Play audio example 2-39




A chord’s sound is as much defined by the chords that are around it in the progression, just as much
as the notes of the chord themselves.



 Exercise: notice what happens to the chord symbol of the following chord if we consider a
 different context for this group of notes. The same group of notes below could imply 3 different
 chords – and many more besides!




   Play audio example 2-40


Finding More Interesting Voicings

In this lesson, let’s look at an easy way to generate more chord voicings from the ones you have
already learned – which will help us get more creativewhen we tackle chord melody arranging in the
next module.

Let’s take this plain G7 voicing:




   Play audio example 2-41


Notice how I can turn this into a new voicing by simply adding a note – in this case, an A on the 1st
string with my 3rd finger:




   Play audio example 2-42


You can get very creative with this simple idea. Notice how many different chord voicings I can create
from this G7 shape, simply by adding or adapting notes from the basic shape:




   Play audio example 2-43


Hint: The number below each voicing indicates the interval of the top note of the voicing, which is
very useful to know for chord melody arranging as you’ll see in future lessons!



 Exercise: take any chord voicings you have already learned. Experiment adding notes, removing
 notes, or raising/lowering notes in the voicing to create new sounds. As an extra challenge, see if
 you can work out what the chord name would be for the new voicings you have created.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
